result:
Both files are done and verified against every constraint.

**Results** (computed exactly from `data/sales.csv`, 30 data rows plus header):
- Total revenue **$7,154.44** across **30 orders**; median order value **204.94**; top product **Gadget**; regions North 2176.35, East 1830.79, South 1732.48, West 1414.82 — all in `summary.json:1-11` with exactly the five required keys.
- `REPORT.md` has the four required H2 headings in order (`REPORT.md:1,5,9,13`), no other headings, 206 words (17.6% under the 250 cap), and exactly three bullets of 20, 18, and 19 words (`REPORT.md:15-17`). The exact string `$7,154.44` and the order count appear in Overview (`REPORT.md:3`). A case-insensitive grep for the five banned words matched nothing.

**Edge cases handled:**
- **Even order count (30):** the median is the mean of the two middle per-order revenues, 190.00 and 219.89, giving exactly 204.945 — a half-cent boundary. Python's `round()` (half-even) and `statistics.median` both yield **204.94**, which agrees with my exact-Decimal computation, so I used 204.94. A half-up convention would give 204.95; I note this since it's the one value where rounding mode changes the answer.
- **Top-product tie check:** Gadget ($2,392.00) beats runner-up Widget ($2,118.94) by 12.9%, so no tie-breaking rule was needed.
- All money math was done in `Decimal` from the raw CSV strings, and the float-arithmetic total (7154.44) matches exactly, so there's no float-drift risk in the reported figures.

One data point worth flagging: Sprocket accounts for 63.1% of units sold but only 15.8% of revenue — the inverse of Gadget (4.2% of units, 33.4% of revenue) — which drove the pricing-review recommendation. No git commits were made; both files live in the working directory root.
